us-politics.headline: US Jets Downed, Iran Threats Escalate as War Drags On
us-politics.body:
US military aircraft have been shot down by enemy fire for the first time in over 20 years during the ongoing conflict with Iran, marking a significant escalation in combat losses. Trump declared the war "near completion" while simultaneously raising new threats against Tehran, a tension that drew scrutiny on Sunday political shows.

Commentators on the right frame Trump as shouldering the burdens of a wartime presidency, arguing the administration is making hard but necessary calls. Critics question the mixed messaging. One analysis argues the US can outlast Iran through attrition without committing ground troops, a strategy that remains contested across party lines.

sources.title: Sunday shows preview: Trump ups threats on Iran after saying war ‘near completion’
TEVI TROY: Trump faces the burdens of a wartime presidency
US military jets hit in Iran war are the first shot down by enemy fire in over 20 years
The US can beat Iran at its own game, and without boots on the ground

us-politics.headline: ICE Arrests Soleimani Relatives; Van Hollen Clash Heats Up
us-politics.body:
ICE arrested relatives of slain Iranian general Qasem Soleimani living in the US after Secretary of State Rubio revoked their green cards, a move the administration frames as a national security measure tied to the Iran conflict. The arrests signal a direct link between the military campaign abroad and immigration enforcement at home.

Separately, DHS pushed back hard against Democratic Senator Chris Van Hollen, who claimed an illegal immigrant caused a crash while fleeing ICE. DHS says the facts contradict his account. The dispute reflects a broader pattern of sharp conflict between the administration and Democratic lawmakers over how ICE operations are conducted and communicated to the public.

sources.title: DHS slams Democrat Sen Chris Van Hollen claim, says illegal alien caused crash while fleeing ICE
ICE arrests relatives of slain Iranian general Soleimani living in US after Rubio revokes their green cards

us-politics.headline: Judge Blocks Trump Admissions Order; Wisconsin Race Signals GOP Trouble
us-politics.body:
A federal judge blocked the Trump administration's attempt to require colleges to certify that race played no role in admissions decisions, a direct challenge to the administration's post-affirmative-action enforcement push. The ruling limits how far the executive branch can go in policing campus compliance without new legislation.

Meanwhile, Wisconsin's state Supreme Court race is exposing cracks in the state Republican Party. The Hill reports the GOP is struggling to consolidate support and messaging in a race that carries national implications for judicial balance in a key swing state. Both stories point to legal and electoral headwinds the right is navigating simultaneously.

sources.title: Judge blocks Trump bid to require colleges to prove race not considered in admissions
Wisconsin judicial race spotlights state GOP’s struggles

us-politics.headline: White House Ballroom Fight, China Approval Surge, and More
us-politics.body:
Trump filed an emergency appeal to keep construction of a White House ballroom moving forward after a court intervened, with questions swirling over the project's legal basis and use of federal space. The dispute is part of a broader pattern of legal friction surrounding the administration's use of executive property.

On the global stage, a new Gallup poll shows China has overtaken the US in international approval ratings, a finding that cuts across partisan lines but lands differently depending on who is reading it. Supporters of the administration attribute the shift to deliberate America-first repositioning. Critics argue it reflects damaged alliances and declining soft power. FBI Director Patel also made headlines with a blunt warning to anyone who attacks law enforcement, promising swift federal consequences.

sources.title: FBI's Patel delivers blunt warning to law enforcement attackers: 'We're going to put you down'
What we know about the White House ballroom bunker
Trump files emergency appeal to keep building White House ballroom
China now tops US in global approval ratings: Gallup

au-politics.headline: Australia scrambles to secure fuel supply amid global disruption
au-politics.body:
Prime Minister Albanese has secured assurances from Japan and other Asian partners that fuel supplies to Australia will continue normally, as the government moves to contain a domestic shortage. Nearly 100 NSW service stations have been fined $1,100 each for displaying misleading prices during the crunch, with authorities urging motorists to report further price gouging. Western Australia is expanding its FuelWatch scheme, requiring all regional stations to report prices from May.

The crackdown reflects growing government concern about both supply security and consumer exploitation. Thousands still attended the National Folk Festival in Canberra despite fuel cost worries, suggesting the shortage has not yet severely disrupted daily life. The diplomatic push and regulatory response signal the Albanese government is treating this as a serious economic and political pressure point.

sources.title: Nearly 100 NSW service stations fined $1,100 each over misleading petrol prices amid fuel shortage crackdown
Asian countries assure Australia ‘normal supply’ of fuel will continue as Albanese focuses on averting shortages
Japan assures Australia will get 'normal supply' of fuel
All WA regional stations must report fuel prices from May as FuelWatch scheme expands
Motorists urged to dob in service stations over fuel price hikes
Thousands flock to National Folk Festival despite fuel cost concerns

au-politics.headline: Melbourne murder arrest, Tasmania shooting, Freeman family sues police
au-politics.body:
A man was arrested at Melbourne Airport attempting to board an international flight and has been charged with murdering a woman. The arrest came before he could leave the country. In Tasmania, a man remains in serious condition after being shot by police in Goodwood.

In Victoria, the sisters of Dezi Freeman have announced they will sue Victoria Police, describing their grief as painful beyond words. Freeman's death and the family's legal action raise fresh questions about police conduct and accountability. The three incidents, unfolding over Easter weekend, put law enforcement under scrutiny across multiple states simultaneously.

sources.title: Man arrested attempting to board international flight at Melbourne airport charged with murdering woman
Man in serious condition after being shot by Tasmania Police
'Painful beyond words': Sisters of Dezi Freeman to sue Victoria Police

au-politics.headline: Victoria's First Nations treaty elections mark historic milestone
au-politics.body:
Victoria's First Nations treaty body is holding its inaugural elections, a landmark step in the state's treaty process. The elections give Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander communities a formal say in negotiations with the state government. Advocates say the process centres Indigenous people as experts in their own lives and governance.

The development comes as treaty and Indigenous rights remain contested nationally, following the failed federal Voice referendum. Victoria has pressed ahead with its own framework regardless. The elections represent the most concrete institutional progress on treaty in any Australian state to date.

sources.title: ‘We’re the experts in our own lives’: Victoria’s First Nations treaty body is holding its first elections

au-politics.headline: Australians mark Easter amid quake, cyclone recovery and celebrations
au-politics.body:
Australians celebrated Easter across a backdrop of disruption and resilience. A magnitude-5.5 earthquake rattled the APY Lands in South Australia early Sunday, described by locals as a jarring morning wake-up call. In Queensland, the North West Coastal Highway reopened after Cyclone Narelle, allowing Easter road trips to resume.

Despite the disruptions, communities gathered for Easter events nationwide, with ABC reporting a broad mood of finding joy amid global uncertainty. The contrast between natural hazards and public celebration captured a recurring tension in Australian life - ordinary rhythms continuing alongside genuine risk.

sources.title: Australians find joy amid global uncertainty in Easter celebrations
Travellers celebrate as major highway reopens after Cyclone Narelle
Magnitude-5.5 earthquake 'morning wake-up call' to remote community

world.headline: US-Israel War on Iran Escalates With Strikes, Rescues, Arrests
world.body:
US and Israeli forces have struck Iranian universities and civilian infrastructure, including a Tehran psychiatric hospital rendered unusable by the attacks. Trump confirmed the rescue of a US officer shot down over Iran, saying only "we got him" - while the US arrested relatives of slain Iranian general Qasem Soleimani, a move Tehran called hostage-taking.

Iran is pushing back where it can. It fired on a Kuwaiti oil complex and rejected Trump's ultimatum, while granting Iraqi ships passage through the Strait of Hormuz as transit traffic climbs. US satellite firm Planet Labs announced a blackout on imagery of the conflict zone, limiting independent documentation of the strikes.

The war is rippling outward. Italy's Meloni flew to Qatar to secure energy supplies. Pakistan is navigating serious economic and diplomatic fallout. Polls show the conflict is deeply unpopular with the American public.

sources.title: Trump’s unpopular war on Iran
Iran says Iraqi ships can pass Strait of Hormuz as transits tick up
US satellite firm Planet Labs announces blackout on war on Iran images
Iran war live: Trump confirms rescue of US officer, says ‘we got him’
US says it has arrested relatives of late Iranian ​general Qasem ​Soleimani
How will Pakistan deal with the fallout from Iran war?
What we know so far about the US fighter jet shot down over Iran
Tehran psychiatric hospital not usable after US-Israeli strike
Universities hit as US, Israel ramp up attacks on Iran’s infrastructure
Iran’s coastline: architecture of control
Italy’s Meloni meets Qatar emir to discuss energy issues amid Iran war

world.headline: Gaza and West Bank: Israeli Strikes and Mosque Restrictions Continue
world.body:
Israeli drone strikes continued in central Gaza, with footage showing vehicles engulfed in flames. In the West Bank, Israeli authorities have tightened restrictions on the Ibrahimi Mosque in Hebron, limiting Palestinian access to one of Islam's holiest sites through a combination of checkpoints, permit requirements, and extended closure periods.

In Tel Aviv, Israeli police arrested antiwar protesters, underscoring growing domestic dissent over the government's military campaigns. Indonesian peacekeepers killed in southern Lebanon were repatriated, a reminder that the region's conflicts are claiming lives well beyond Gaza's borders.

sources.title: Israeli measures tighten grip on Hebron’s Ibrahimi Mosque
Indonesia receives bodies of peacekeepers killed in southern Lebanon
Israeli police arrest antiwar protesters in Tel Aviv
Vehicle engulfed in flames after Israeli drone strike in central Gaza

world.headline: Germany Eyes Military Travel Curbs for Young Men
world.body:
Germany is considering requiring men under 45 to obtain military approval before taking extended trips abroad, a proposal that reflects Berlin's accelerating shift toward wartime-style defence planning. The measure would apply to those of conscription age and marks one of the most concrete steps any NATO country has taken to prepare its population for potential mobilisation.

Separately, Russia killed five people in a strike on a Ukrainian market, the latest in a string of attacks on civilian areas. In Estonia, rumours of a separatist "People's Republic" in the Russian-speaking city of Narva have circulated online, though local officials and analysts say the claims are unfounded and likely part of an information operation.

sources.title: 'People's Republic' rumors: Does an Estonian city really want to join Russia?
German males under 45 may need military approval for long stays abroad
Russian attack on Ukraine market kills five

world.headline: White House Ballroom Fight, Louisiana Parade Crash, and More
world.body:
Trump filed an emergency legal motion to continue construction of a ballroom inside the White House grounds, a project blocked by a federal judge citing historic preservation rules. The administration argues the renovation is a matter of presidential authority. A man was charged in New York after fatally shooting a baby in a pram.

In Louisiana, a car ploughed into a Lao New Year parade, injuring around 15 people. Cambodia unveiled a statue honouring Magawa, a giant African rat trained to detect landmines who saved countless lives before his death. The Giuffre family urged King Charles to meet Jeffrey Epstein survivors during his upcoming US visit.

sources.title: Cambodia unveils statue to honour famous landmine-sniffing rat
ICE wanted to build a detention centre -  this small farming town said no
Man charged over fatal shooting of baby in pram in New York
Car slams into Louisiana Lao New Year parade, injuring about 15 people
Giuffre family urges UK’s King Charles to meet Epstein survivors in US
Trump files emergency motion to continue building White House ballroom

ai.headline: AI Copyright Chaos: Artists Fight Back Against Their Own Clones
ai.body:
A folk musician named Murphy Campbell is the latest artist caught in a nightmare loop: AI trained on his work generated fake versions of his music, then a copyright troll filed claims against him using that AI output. The story, reported by The Verge, illustrates how the legal system can be weaponized against the very creators whose work fed these models.

The absurdity is compounding. One AI system reportedly filed a copyright claim against the original artist whose files it copied, a reversal that would have seemed like satire two years ago. It no longer does. The creative community has no clean legal remedy yet, and the trolls know it.

sources.title: AI that copied musical artist files copyright claim against that artist
A folk musician became a target for AI fakes and a copyright troll

ai.headline: Developers Push Back on AI Coding Tools - With Mixed Feelings
ai.body:
A wave of developer writing this week captures a genuine tension: AI coding assistants work, and many programmers resent that. One engineer detailed using AI successfully on a project while feeling a creeping unease about what the experience meant for his craft. Separately, a Lisp developer noted that the language's structural quirks make it largely resistant to AI assistance - and found himself conflicted about whether that was a feature or a bug.

Meanwhile, Anthropic announced Claude Code subscribers will need to pay extra for OpenClaw support, adding pricing friction to a tool already generating debate. Andrej Karpathy published an example "idea file" for LLM workflows, signaling that even AI insiders are still figuring out how to integrate these tools thoughtfully. The honeymoon phase is clearly over.

sources.title: I used AI. It worked. I hated it
Writing Lisp is AI resistant and I'm sad
LLM Wiki – example of an "idea file"
Anthropic says Claude Code subscribers will need to pay extra for OpenClaw usage

ai.headline: Claude Code Leak Spreads Online - Now Bundled With Malware
ai.body:
Hackers are distributing copies of the leaked Claude Code across the internet, and they have added a bonus: malware embedded in the packages. Wired reports the poisoned files are circulating in communities where developers might plausibly seek out the leaked tool, making the threat particularly targeted.

The incident lands at an awkward moment for Anthropic, which is simultaneously managing the leak fallout, announcing new pricing for Claude Code, and publishing research on how LLMs represent emotion concepts internally. Security researchers are warning developers to treat any unofficial Claude Code distribution as compromised until proven otherwise.

sources.title: Hackers Are Posting the Claude Code Leak With Bonus Malware
Anthropic says Claude Code subscribers will need to pay extra for OpenClaw usage
Emotion concepts and their function in a large language model

ai.headline: Right-to-Repair and Digital ID Face Industry Pushback
ai.body:
Colorado's right-to-repair law, one of the strongest in the US, is under coordinated attack from tech companies lobbying to carve out exemptions before it takes full effect. Ars Technica reports the industry push targets provisions that would force manufacturers to provide parts and software access to independent repair shops.

In Europe, Germany's implementation of the eIDAS digital identity standard has drawn criticism for a different reason: the architecture requires users to have an Apple or Google account to function, effectively handing two American platform companies a gatekeeping role over European digital identity infrastructure. Both stories share the same shape - governments pass protections, and industry finds the seams.

sources.title: Tech companies are trying to neuter Colorado’s landmark right-to-repair law
German implementation of eIDAS will require an Apple/Google account to function

btc.headline: Bitcoin Sentiment Turns Bearish as Markets Stagnate
btc.body:
Bearish chatter around Bitcoin has hit a five-week high, according to Santiment data, as price action remains flat and retail enthusiasm fades. That kind of sentiment buildup often precedes a move - historically, peak pessimism has marked local bottoms rather than sustained downturns.

Research from Mercado Bitcoin adds context: Bitcoin has consistently outperformed gold and equities in the months following major global shocks. The pattern suggests that short-term sentiment and long-term performance frequently diverge. Traders watching social media noise may be reading the wrong signal.

sources.title: Bitcoin reaches highest level of bearish chatter in 5 weeks: Santiment
Bitcoin tends to outperform gold and stocks after global shocks, Mercado Bitcoin finds

btc.headline: Bitcoin's Quantum Threat: A $1.3 Trillion Security Problem
btc.body:
Bitcoin's cryptographic foundations face a credible long-term threat from quantum computing, and developers are racing to respond. CoinDesk outlines several active initiatives aimed at making the network's signature schemes resistant to quantum attacks - a challenge complicated by Bitcoin's deliberately slow, consensus-driven upgrade process.

Jimmy Song's call for a more conservative node client fits the same broader concern. Song argues that Bitcoin's resilience depends on clients that resist feature creep and prioritize stability. Together, these debates reflect a network grappling with how to evolve without compromising the properties that make it valuable.

sources.title: Bitcoin's $1.3 trillion security race: Key initiatives aimed at quantum-proofing the world's largest blockchain
Jimmy Song explains why Bitcoin needs a 'conservative' node client

btc.headline: Iran Bans Telegram. It Doesn't Work.
btc.body:
Pavel Durov says Iran's government ban on Telegram backfired, driving more users to the platform rather than away from it. The claim tracks with a well-documented pattern - state-level blocks on encrypted messaging apps tend to accelerate adoption among the populations they target.

For the crypto industry, Telegram remains a primary organizing layer for communities, token projects, and trading groups. Any sustained crackdown on the platform in major markets carries real operational implications for how crypto projects communicate and coordinate.

sources.title: Telegram founder Pavel Durov says Iranian government's ban backfired

food.name: Manti
food.description: Tiny hand-pinched dumplings filled with spiced lamb, drowned in garlicky yogurt and chilli butter
food.tip: Make the dumplings thumb-nail small — the ratio of dough to filling is what makes or breaks this dish, and smaller means better texture per bite

exercise.name: Sunday Staircase Pyramid
exercise.equipment: A staircase of at least 10 steps
exercise.description: Sprint up the stairs one step at a time, walk down, then two steps at a time up, walk down, repeating the pyramid pattern while tracking heart rate recovery between sets

learn.topic: The Ship of Theseus and Personal Identity
learn.description: The ancient paradox asks whether an object that has had all its components replaced remains fundamentally the same object — and when applied to the human body, which replaces most of its cells over years, it becomes a sharp question about what 'you' actually are across time.
learn.why: Derek Parfit's modern take in Reasons and Persons pushes this into genuinely unsettling territory around memory, continuity, and why we fear death — an hour here will quietly rewire how you think about yourself for weeks
